# Badge

Displays a compact label for status, category, or metadata.

## Contents

- [Installation](#installation)
- [Usage](#usage)
- [Variants](#variants)
- [Status versus action](#status-versus-action)
- [Accessibility and live status](#accessibility-and-live-status)

## Installation

Add the Badge component from the configured StackTrace registry.

```shell
npx shadcn-vue@latest add @stacktrace/badge
```

## Usage

Use a badge for a short value that supplements nearby content, such as a status, category, version, or count. Keep the label concise and make sure the surrounding text still explains what the value belongs to.

**Badge example**

```vue
<template>
  <Badge>Badge</Badge>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Badge } from '@/Components/Badge'
</script>
```

## Variants

Use `default` when the label needs the most emphasis, `secondary` for ordinary metadata, `outline` for a quiet category, and `destructive` for a negative status. Choose by meaning and emphasis, not by alternating colors for decoration.

**Secondary example**

```vue
<template>
  <Badge variant="secondary">Badge</Badge>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Badge } from '@/Components/Badge'
</script>
```

**Outline example**

```vue
<template>
  <Badge variant="outline">Badge</Badge>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Badge } from '@/Components/Badge'
</script>
```

**Destructive example**

```vue
<template>
  <Badge variant="destructive">Badge</Badge>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Badge } from '@/Components/Badge'
</script>
```

### Custom status colors

The supported variants are default, secondary, destructive, and outline. A stable product vocabulary may add utility classes for a positive or warning status. Use the same color and wording consistently across the application, and never rely on color alone.

**Positive example**

```vue
<template>
  <Badge class="border-transparent bg-emerald-600 text-white">
    Positive
  </Badge>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Badge } from '@/Components/Badge'
</script>
```

**Warning example**

```vue
<template>
  <Badge class="border-transparent bg-amber-500 text-amber-950">
    Warning
  </Badge>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Badge } from '@/Components/Badge'
</script>
```

## Status versus action

A badge is a label by default, not a button. Do not make a passive status look clickable or use a badge in place of a primary action. If selecting a compact value changes state, use Toggle or Toggle Group; if it performs a command, use Button.

### Links

Badge forwards Reka Primitive composition props. Use `as-child` when a short category or version should genuinely navigate, preserving the child link's semantics. Give the link enough context to make its destination clear.

```vue
<template>
  <Badge as-child variant="secondary">
    <Link href="/releases/v2.7.4">Version 2.7.4</Link>
  </Badge>
</template>

<script setup lang="ts">
import { Link } from '@inertiajs/vue3'
import { Badge } from '@/Components/Badge'
</script>
```

## Accessibility and live status

Write meaningful text such as “Failed” or “3 unread” instead of relying on a colored dot. Badge does not create a live region, so a dynamically changed badge is not automatically announced. Add a separate concise status message when an update must be announced; avoid placing a rapidly changing count in `aria-live`.

> **Use Alert for an explanatory message: [Alert](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/components/alert)**
>
> When the user needs a title, explanation, or recovery guidance, use an Alert rather than stretching a compact badge into a message container.

Hosted documentation: [Badge](https://ui.stacktrace.sk/docs/components/badge)
